result:
The autumn leaves are falling again, each one a small death, a quiet surrender. I used to think of this season as melancholy, a slow fading toward winter’s blank page. But lately I’ve started to see it differently. There’s a strange brilliance in the way the trees let go, a riot of color that erupts only because the chlorophyll is retreating. The green masks a hidden palette, and only when the life-sustaining energy withdraws do the true pigments emerge—the golds, the ambers, the deep crimsons. It’s as if the leaves are finally showing who they were all along, right at the moment they release their hold on the branch.

I think we’re not so different. So much of our lives is spent clinging—to plans, to identities, to people, to the way things were. We brace against change like a leaf fighting the autumn wind, forgetting that the letting go is written into the very fabric of existence. There’s a Japanese phrase, *mono no aware*, the bittersweet awareness of impermanence. It suggests that beauty is heightened by the knowledge that it must pass. A cherry blossom is more exquisite because it blooms so briefly. A friendship is more precious because time will inevitably reshape it.

Maybe the falling leaf isn’t a symbol of loss but of completion. It has done its work, photosynthesizing light into nourishment, and now it returns to the earth, not as an ending but as a contribution to the soil from which new things will grow. There’s a profound trust in that. The tree doesn’t hoard its leaves, doesn’t panic about the lean months ahead. It simply lets go, knowing that spring is a promise written deep in its roots. And standing here, watching the slow, spiraling descent, I realize I’m not just witnessing an end. I’m watching a necessary part of a larger rhythm, a rehearsal for all the letting go I’ve yet to learn.
